title: Romans 1:16-17
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro

outline.title: I. The Righteousness of God Is Being Revealed (Romans 1:17a)
subpoints: The righteousness of God is being revealed — present passive indicative — every time the Scriptures are preached
The righteousness of God is the righteousness that comes from God, declared to be ours in Christ
God requires perfection — there is none righteous, no, not one (Romans 3:10); 1 John 1:5-6
The gospel offers the solution: God provides the righteousness we lack through Christ
This 'alien righteousness' is outside ourselves — a foreign righteousness found only in the gospel

outline.title: II. Righteousness Comes Entirely by Faith (Romans 1:17b)
subpoints: From faith to faith — faith in the beginning, faith in the end, faith all the way through
Christ became flesh to meet God's standard on our behalf
His earthly life obeyed every aspect of the law of God in our place — perfectly
His death was not the great punishment — separation from the Father was: 'My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me?'
His resurrection guarantees our newness of life and proves the Father accepted His sacrifice
God the Father declares Christ's righteousness to be ours through faith — justification by faith alone

outline.title: III. The Double Imputation
subpoints: Christ's righteous life is imputed to us — counted as ours through faith
Our sin is imputed to Christ — the cross is God declaring His Son guilty so His people can be innocent
Mel Gibson's hands nailing Christ: representative of what we all did
No other religion offers what God provides for free — every religion requires you to earn your way
The doctrine of justification by faith alone in Christ alone by grace alone

outline.title: IV. The Gospel Is the Power of God for All Salvation (Romans 1:16)
subpoints: Salvation refers to the entirety of redemption: past (justified), present (being sanctified), future (glorified)
Romans 8:24 — saved in hope; 1 Corinthians 1:18 — being saved; Romans 5:9 — shall be saved from wrath
The gospel is powerful to save everyone who believes — universal in offer, faith as the only condition
Both Jew and Greek — only two kinds of people; salvation is available to all
Acts 4:12: no other name under heaven by which we must be saved

outline.title: V. Paul Is Not Ashamed of the Gospel — Are We?
subpoints: Peter's denial of Christ illustrates shame of the gospel (Matthew 26:69-75)
We deny Jesus to protect ourselves from social embarrassment or loss of prestige
We are ashamed because we lack faith that the gospel actually works
Mark 8:38 — whoever is ashamed of Me and My words, of him will the Son of Man be ashamed
May the Lord convince us of the power of the gospel so we will not be ashamed of it

summary:
Pastor Tito Lyro preaches a standalone sermon on Romans 1:16-17, which he describes as the thematic foundation of the entire epistle. He begins at the end of verse 17 and works backwards, building from the ground up. The righteousness of God — a present, ongoing revelation every time Scripture is preached — is the alien righteousness that is outside ourselves, the righteousness that comes from God and is declared to be ours in Christ. The sermon expounds justification by faith alone: Christ became flesh to obey the law perfectly on our behalf, He died the death sinners deserved (most profoundly in His separation from the Father), and He rose again to guarantee life to those He represented. The double imputation — Christ's righteousness counted to us, our guilt counted to Him — is the heart of the gospel.

Pastor Lyro then turns to Paul's statement about not being ashamed, using Peter's denial of Christ as a mirror. Peter denied Jesus to avoid the social consequences of association with Him, and we do the same — in more sophisticated ways — whenever we fail to share the gospel out of fear of embarrassment, lost prestige, or social disapproval. The sermon ends with a direct challenge: are we ashamed of the gospel? Jesus warns that whoever is ashamed of Him, of him the Son of Man will be ashamed when He comes in His Father's glory. The only remedy is being convinced by the Spirit of the power of this gospel — the power of God unto salvation for everyone who believes.
transcript:
## Introduction

You don't have to worry — this is not the beginning of a series in Romans. It's just a standalone that goes along with what we see in 2 Corinthians. The Lord in His providence — we didn't have a service last week — has in mind for us this sermon today as a follow-up on what Craig taught in Sunday School.

This morning we're going to read two verses of Romans chapter 1, two precious verses, verses 16 and 17.

For I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ, for it is the power of God to salvation for everyone who believes, for the Jew first and also for the Greek. For in it the righteousness of God is revealed from faith to faith; as it is written, the just shall live by faith.

Let us pray together. Father in heaven, thank You that You are a good God. We thank You for the gospel. We pray that it will be clear to us this afternoon. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen.

## Context

The Apostle tells us in 2 Corinthians that he is dying for the sake of the gospel, and that the gospel is a super-abundant power of God.

We like powerful things. We like strong things. That's why the Olympics are such an attraction. Every four years we are superfans of all those sports we've never heard of before. Well, in this passage, we have the most powerful thing at work in our lives — the gospel of the Lord Jesus Christ.

Paul tells us in verse 15 that he's ready to preach at Rome. He had never been to Rome before — not a church he started — but he's going there for the first time. Eventually, in God's providence, he gets there, but as a prisoner. God still fulfilled his request to preach in Rome.

So he's ready to preach the gospel in Rome because he is not ashamed of the gospel. And Paul is not ashamed of it because it is the power of God into salvation. He's confident that the gospel is the power of God into salvation because in it the righteousness of God is revealed.

Because that last truth is really the foundation of Paul's argument in the whole book of Romans, let's start from the end and work our way up.

## I. The Righteousness of God Is Being Revealed

The righteousness of God is revealed. Literally, it's being revealed — a passive present indicative, if that makes any sense. It's going on right now. The righteousness of God is being revealed.

We think that was already revealed in the pages of the Bible in the Old and New Testaments. And it is true that all things concerning the righteousness of God have been infallibly revealed in the pages of the Bible. But it is also being made known — being revealed — every time the Scriptures are preached. So every Sunday, every time we gather together to hear from the Lord, the righteousness of God is being revealed to us.

What is the righteousness of God? It is the righteousness that comes from God — a righteousness that finds its origin in God — the righteousness that God declares to be ours in Jesus Christ, which finds its source in Him.

Now what does it mean to be righteous? To be righteous is to be perfect. It is to be just. It is to have met the standard set before us. We know that we haven't done that. In Romans 3:10, Paul says there is none righteous, no, not one. And yet God requires nothing short of perfection. In 1 John 1:5-6, John says, God is light, and in Him is no darkness at all. If we say that we have fellowship with Him and walk in darkness, we lie and do not practice the truth. God requires that we walk in light every single second, every single nanosecond, every single day.

So we have a very serious problem. We must approach God and worship Him and serve Him if we want to live, but He can only be approached, worshiped, and served by the righteous ones.

That's why the gospel comes in. The gospel offers the solution. God provides the righteousness that we lack and so desperately need to be in fellowship with Him. It is in the gospel that we discover this alien righteousness — that is, a righteousness outside of ourselves, a foreign righteousness. And we find that in the gospel of Jesus Christ.

In Romans 3:21-22: Now the righteousness of God, apart from the law, is revealed, being witnessed by the law and the prophets. Even the righteousness of God which is through faith in Jesus Christ, to all and on all who believe.

In Romans 5:17: For if by one man's offense death reigns through the one, much more those who receive abundance of grace and of the gift of righteousness will reign in life through the one, Jesus Christ.

We are not righteous. We're far from it. But God in Christ provides all the righteousness we need to be in fellowship with Him.

## II. This Righteousness Comes Entirely by Faith

Notice in Romans 1:17 that this righteousness is apart from the law — apart from our own accomplishments and efforts to obey God. And notice also that it is a righteousness that comes entirely by faith in Jesus Christ. The Apostle says it is revealed from faith to faith — faith in the beginning, faith in the end, faith ever in between.

How does that work? God's Son became flesh like one of us so that He could meet God's standard on our behalf. Everything that Christ did pleased God. And nothing that He did displeased God — both in the negative and the positive. He precisely kept everything, without being arrogant, without being prideful, without being condescending to other people.

After having done all that — which He didn't need to do because He was already inherently righteous — His reward was death. The great punishment was actually not the physical crucifixion but the separation from the Father. When He cries out, My God, My God, why have You forsaken Me? He died the death the sinners deserved on behalf of His people. Then He came back to life to give life to those whom He represented. The resurrection tells us that we have newness of life in Christ, and that the Father accepted the sacrifice of the Son.

He did all that as a man, as the representative man, as the second Adam, as the federal head of a people. And as we believe that Christ did that for us, God the Father declares that the righteousness Christ secured with His life and His death is our righteousness. When the Father looks at you — positionally, because you are in Christ — He sees a life of perfection. He's counting what was Christ's as now yours. Not because you're a good boy or girl, but because you believe in the Lord Jesus Christ.

## III. The Double Imputation

Christ's righteousness is imputed to us — counted as ours — but that's only half of the equation.

The other side is that we also contribute something to the equation. Oh no, the pastor is going off the deep end — he says we contribute to our salvation! We do. The other side is that all the guilt of our sins is counted to Christ. That's what the crucifixion is.

There's one scene in The Passion of the Christ where Mel Gibson's own hands are seen nailing the nails through the hands of Christ. He made sure his hands were the ones doing it — in recognition that it is representative of what we all did. It is my sin, your sin, the guilt of our sins, that nailed Him to the cross, that separated Him from His Father. Because all our sins — yesterday's, today's, tomorrow's — were counted to Him. In the courts of heaven, God declared: My Son is guilty, so that His people can be innocent.

That's why in 2 Corinthians 5:21, the apostle says, for He made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us, that we might become the righteousness of God in Him.

And that is what we call the doctrine of justification by faith alone, in Christ alone.

Do you realize that no other religion offers this? No other religion teaches that God provides all you need to be able to be with Him. Every other religion — Jehovah's Witnesses, Mormons, Muslims, environmentalism, whatever — teaches that you must do something on your own to reach up to God. The problem is that there's never anything good enough. But God does it for us.

## IV. The Gospel Is the Power of God for Salvation

Since it is the gospel that God's righteousness is found, it is powerful to save. Verse 16: For I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ, for it is the power of God to salvation for everyone who believes, for the Jew first and also for the Greek.

Salvation here refers to the entirety of redemption — from the moment you are effectually called, all the way through glorification. The gospel is good for all of that from beginning to end.

Romans 8:24: For we were saved in this hope. 1 Corinthians 1:18: The message of the cross to us who are being saved is the power of God. Romans 5:9: Much more then, having now been justified by His blood, we shall be saved from wrath through Him. That's future salvation — final vindication.

Brothers and sisters, no matter what you're going through, you are never going to experience the wrath of God. Whatever contrary providences you're going through — He's not punishing you in wrath, because His wrath for you was poured upon Christ.

So the gospel is the power of God in every area of existence. From the moment we're born again, to the moment we rise from the dead to enjoy Christ forever — it's all Christ, it's all faith, and it's all grace.

And the gospel is powerful to save everyone who believes. It is universal in its offer. Anyone who believes will be saved. There's no other way to be saved, and if the nations are going to be saved, they'll be saved through the gospel. Paul says it is the power to the Jews first and then also for the Greeks — only two kinds of people. If you fall into one of those two groups — that's everyone — salvation is available to you. But only through Jesus Christ. As Peter said: Nor is there salvation in any other, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved.

The man on an island in the Pacific will not be saved unless he believes. He will not believe unless he hears. He will not hear without a preacher. He will not have a preacher unless we who believe proclaim it. This is also true of the person downtown or the family living next door to us.

## V. Are We Ashamed of the Gospel?

Now, man — why does Paul even bring this up? Why start the greatest theological book ever written with a statement about not being ashamed of the gospel? Because we have a tendency to be ashamed of the gospel.

How are we ashamed? Let me ask you to turn to Matthew 26. This is where we find Peter's denial of the Lord.

Matthew 26:69-75: Now Peter sat outside in the courtyard. And a servant girl came to him, saying, You also were with Jesus of Galilee. But he denied it before them all, saying, I do not know what you are saying. And when he had gone out to the gateway, another girl saw him and said to those who were there, This fellow also was with Jesus of Nazareth. But again he denied with an oath, I do not know the man. And a little later those who stood by came up and said to Peter, Surely you also are one of them, for your speech betrays you. Then he began to curse and swear, saying, I do not know the man.

We may be more sophisticated in our shame. We may say things like: the Spirit didn't move, or the right opportunity didn't present itself, or I need to pick my battles. But essentially the same shame is there that we find in Peter.

Why did Peter deny Jesus? He denied Jesus because he was ashamed of Jesus. He denied Jesus to protect himself from harmful consequences that his association with Jesus could bring. How often we don't share the gospel because we're afraid of losing prestige, or of being lumped together with those considered lunatics in society. How often we're ashamed because we're not sure if this gospel thing really works — we lack faith.

Do you know what Jesus says to all this? Whoever is ashamed of Me and My words in this adulterous and sinful generation, of him the Son of Man also will be ashamed when He comes in the glory of His Father with the holy angels.

May the Lord convince us of the power of the gospel so that we will not be ashamed of it, because it is — and nothing else is — God's power to salvation.

Let's pray together. Father in heaven, we thank You for Your word and the gospel of Jesus Christ. We pray that You would convince us of it, that we would not be ashamed of it. We thank You that the only thing we bring to this relationship is our sin, and everything else is Yours. We pray these things in Jesus' name. Amen.
